speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This conversation with Ione Adair took place at her home in Moscow on November 16th 1976 her sister Bernadine Adair Cornelison also took part in a conversation The interviewer was Sam Schrager

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Uncle Sam Owings It used to run the Times Democrat here in Moscow years and years ago and He was loved fishing And he wanted to go fishing with us when we went fishing So my sister and I decided we'd break down once and take him with us We took him up to Elk River and he cut side of the bridge the old bridge over the river and He sat down with his pole with that bridge and fish like you would for catfish in in Carolina that's where he was from and He sat there all afternoon and fished in that bridge and Marge and I fished up and down the stream and we had a nice basket full of Fish between us when he got through was coming in Uncle Sam decided he'd sing for us and We'd rather have him sing then smoke those old cigars that he smoked So we told him to sing for us we asked him if he knew any song oh, yes, he said I know it's song so we started in before we left Voville to sing and he sang verse after verse after verse till we landed down here and put him on the lot where the Employment agency is now that was their their home And we let him out there with an empty basket and Song from mobile what was the song he he you told me that one I told you once but I can't remember the name of this song It had burst after person but verse after verse I think he must have made it up things he went along He didn't have been a song that long he didn't catch any fish and on the no just that old southern layer He didn't catch any fish sitting on the bridge catch any fish you think he'd learn from you how to do it He'd want to go up and down the street.

speaker: Ione Adair
words: He didn't like to move around much He's well he had smoked in Carolina And he knew he knew how to fish in Carolina for those they were catfish I think You had a fish there but he didn't know anything about going up and down the stream and watching the holes and watching for the fish and catching him when they got on Didn't know a thing about that he just waited for the fish to come and take his bait and If they got on and got caught why he had a fish.

Yes, it was a limit at 15 fish There was no size limit. You could have any size you wanted any size you could catch There was no No limit to fishing was it your father that taught you how to fish out there Yes, because you'd mentioned to me that you went on the stream with him right I always went with him waiting when he wanted to Go fishing. I'd go with him whether any of the rest of them would go or not. I always went with my father fishing and We always caught all of our fish that we needed and daddy and dr Hatfield fish for years up and down that old Elk River stream they knew every hole and I think they knew all the trout by name And if he caught Johnny today, we'd catch James tomorrow But we drive up we'd leave here early in the morning so to get up there just about daybreak about the time the Sun was up and we'd start fishing and we were always through early and We had great times fishing up and down there my father had Waste Waiters and it came on to your feet rubber and came up above his waistline and then he got a suit for me and He'd take one hole and I'd take another or if she's a big one. We'd both take the hole But a disturbance in two places would cause the fish to bite a little lower a little farther down and When we were fishing with angler worms, we did it that way and we're fishing with grasshoppers We had to get out and spank our grasshoppers while it was Damp in the morning before they began to get lively they are always very stupid at night and As soon as it gets cold the grasshopper will fold up his wings and stay right down in next to the ground where it's warmer And then they spring as it comes up as the warm air comes on then they get up and climb up on the on the grass and you take a flat piece of shingle or Anything other way or a flyswatter if you have one They're the best things to catch them with and we'd swap those Grasshoppers and put them in our bait can and then swap the next one and put him in the bed. He used to raise worms He did A mulch down here.

They would be so meaty and backbone that was so meaty. We just would love to have those bought in from and then daddy had a barrel. So you ate pretty well Well I was a born then. We lived well. We lived well. I can remember one thing. My mother had wanted a big kettle. She didn't bring any cooking utensils or anything from Kansas with her because that was too hard to pack. Dad's books and his operating apparel and all was all he could take care of. So she cut down on everything and she wanted a big kettle in which to boil meat. Dad went down to the secondhand store and he found the big iron kettle about that high. It had been at one time an enamel kettle. The enamel, white enamel around on the inside. Dad didn't want to use it until he had that enamel checked off so that he could scrub it and be sure that it was sterile inside. So when I'd come home from school I was put down with a chisel and a hammer. I sat there and chipped away at the enamel on this old kettle. That's where she put all the portions. So that it satisfied him that he could get it sterile. And it was sterile. Hard work too. Yes, it was hard work. I bet you had a lot of time. I chipped away on it. And now I have an aluminum kettle and I've been trying my best to get the deposit, the water deposit, out of that aluminum kettle and I can't get it out. I used a screwdriver, a putty knife and everything and I chipped away at that. Eventually, I suppose if I don't put a hole through the tea kettle I'll get it clean.

words: It's a swelling in your tonsils on the inside and in an acute Quinzae attack breaks your tonsils up to an enormous size and then they finally gather and break like a boil and so I had had so much Quinzae trouble and Dad didn't want me drinking coffee and yet I wanted something hot to drink. At that time it was a cereal and it came in a piece board carton paper and I got to drinking it and Dad said let me try that. So he tried it and he didn't dislike Postum especially if he used a little cream in it and he could use sugar in his but I didn't care for the sugar so I didn't use sugar. Well, we went that way for quite a little while in the winter and Mother said I'm tired of making two pots of coffee. For breakfast. She said I'm going to drink Postum. So she started in on Postum. Up until that time Mother had had periods of sick headaches every so often she'd get one of these raging sick headaches and they didn't think about it being coffee that was making the headaches. And after Mother had taken Postum for a short time her headaches began to disappear and she kept on drinking Postum with Dad and me. She didn't have any more sick headaches. So she got over the sick headaches.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why didn't your father want you to drink coffee? Was it thought to be bad for you?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Yes. I don't think that was it. We just never had coffee. After I had the Quincy attacks so many times I began to get rheumatism in the knees and a rheumatic condition. And so they decided that if I liked Postum it'd be better for me than coffee. And so I got along with Postum. We had an old attorney in Moscow. JC Elder that had the offices right next to Dad's up overhead of the Creighton building. The next big window next to that was Dad's window and JC Elder's window. Mrs. Elder was gone one winter and I was having an attack to Postum. Quincy. Quincy. And he came and sat with me so that Mother and Dad could get their rest and he spent the night with me. If I needed a drink I had a drink and if I needed anything he was there to help me. So after I got over it and was able to go back to school, old Mr. Elder came up every noon to the house on Third Street and picked up my lunch and brought it to me at the school. I was placed on the third floor of the old Whitworth School and that was three stories up and too much for me to make up and down to go home and Dad wouldn't allow me to do it. So he, Mr. Elder came up and brought my lunch to me and many a day I went. At that time there was a rheumatic medicine called Antaflogestine. Whether you've heard of it or not, I don't know. It was otherwise Colorado mud. It was a

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Denver mud. Denver mud. It was a medical mud and came in in little tight cans and you took it out like putty and put it on where you were having a pain. And I made those trips up and down the stairs to the third floor of the old Whitworth School with packs of mud, Colorado mud around my knees because my knees were so sore that I couldn't go up and down the stairs without it. And I went till the end of school year with Colorado mud around my knees and old J .C. Elder bringing my lunch up to me so I wouldn't have to make the trip up and down the stairs. He sounds awfully kind. He must have really liked your family, liked you. We adored him too. It did, it bothered him. And I never noticed any trouble with my heart then after that for, oh, quite a long time after I got over with.

He probably did say that. He was always watching but never said anything. He just looked. He never told me.

Sixth Street then it wasn't paved and there were no buildings there and nothing but the courthouse came in there later, but we used to go down Sixth Street on Bob's sleds and go clear down, you go clear across to Main Street on that. You'd stop when you got to Main Street before you'd run into some horse and buggy going down the street. There used to be an old butcher by the name of Weinman, Harry Weinman. And some of his people are living around the country now. I don't know just where they are, but he was a butcher and had a butcher shop about where the True Value or Fox store is and he had a butcher shop there and these after -school youngsters and early evening youngsters would get their coasting parties arranged and we'd coast down the hill and then we'd run across this Main Street to the butcher shop and old Mr. Weinman was always watching for us. He knew we'd be there and he always had a weenie for each one of us and a dill pickle and you got a weenie and a dill pickle for a nickel. They were heated with it. They'd eat the weenie. He'd watch for the youngsters and they came home from school. Always had that pot of hot weenies and a dill pickle for them. And we always stopped and we used to when we lived on Third Street, we used to hitch our sleds on the back of the wood wagons. They always hauled wood in and they'd come in on town and down on Hayes and then down Third and stop along the road trying to sell their wood or delivering wood and we used to hitch our sleds to the back of the wood wagon and get on the sled and drive out way out past where that schoolhouse is now on it as you go out on Mountain View Road and we'd go out past the Mountain View Road and along that low road that goes from there out to the mountains or out to the what do we call it the beach orchards and Idlers Rest if you know where those are. Well, we'd go out that way as long as we wanted to ride and then we'd get off. We'd ask the man to stop and let us off and we'd untie our back and watch for the team coming in to my school and we'd hitch hike on the team coming in and come back home again.

speaker: Ione Adair
words: My father had, I told you, had this office up over here at Creighton store and he sent east and had an electric company. He wanted to give electric treatments for horoscopes. Not horoscopes. Oh, oh. I'd like them to see your bones and all. Rheumatism and arthritis and so forth and he wanted to be able to treat them electrically. And so this company made him an enormous big It's a beautiful thing. battery. Oh, it was longer than that fireplace. Longer than that. And high, it would reach as high as the picture above it. It was an enormous thing and had these great big glass wheels. And the wheels were generated, as you turned, would generate electricity. And then it was connected to a chair and sitting on a platform with the chair insulated underneath the standard so that the electricity didn't leave the chair. And he would put a patient on this chair and then take the different instruments for treating them on the neck or on the head or wherever they needed that he would use the different Well, an Indian came up one day and wanted a treatment. They used to have the court held, Indians held court up overhead at the old post office. And when the Indians were at court and this old fellow was having rheumatism very badly so he came up to dad's office and asked him if he would give him a treatment for rheumatism. So dad gave him the treatment. And the old fellow wanted to know if he would be alright if he brought a friend back. My father said, why yes, he would be alright. So the old Indian went back and after court was out came to the office with four or five Indians. And dad had shown him how if you took hands and took one of the electrodes in your hand that it would go through the bodies of all that they came in contact with. So the Indians all took hands and dad gave them the electrode and then turned on the wheel. And the Indians couldn't let go of each other. And they began to jerk and holler and jerk and holler and dad stepped on the bomb. You could have heard them bomb on main street. Dad said he wondered what kind of a war hoop they thought he was carrying up there. But the Indians were just fascinated with that electric battery. And every time they'd come to court there'd be a bunch of them come up to the office for a treatment. They'd want to get someone that hadn't been there before and hadn't had a hold of their hand and couldn't let go. So they hung on to it. And he treated dozens of them. Well, dad wouldn't take any charge for it. He was getting such a kick out of it. He wouldn't take any charge for it. So the Indians then got to bringing him Indian Jerky. That's the jerk venison. Venison.
